discussion_comments.comment_body: `pyarrow` automatically comes with `datasets`, right? So we don't really need to specify this separately?
It doesn't look like datasets pins any versions of pyarrow, so this may have been needed to ensure a minimum version for the downloader functionality?
`datasets` depends on `pyarrow` and the pin is required to support `hf://` protocol uri which is available since `pyarrow 21.0.0`, see https://github.com/apache/arrow/pull/45089

+[project]
+name = "rustbpe"
+version = "0.1.0"
+description = "A very lightweight Rust library for training a GPT tokenizer"
+readme = "README.md"
+requires-python = ">=3.10"
+
+# when uv/pip invoke maturin through the PEP 517 interface, it always builds in release mode by default.
+[build-system]
+requires = ["maturin>=1.9.6,<2.0"]
+build-backend = "maturin"
+
+[tool.maturin]
+module-name = "rustbpe"
+bindings = "pyo3"
+manifest-path = "Cargo.toml"
+features = ["pyo3/extension-module"]

discussion_comments.commented_code:

discussion_comments.comment_body: Why did you need to add this feature?

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -0,0 +1,17 @@
+[project]
+name = "rustbpe"
+version = "0.1.0"
+description = "A very lightweight Rust library for training a GPT tokenizer"
+readme = "README.md"
+requires-python = ">=3.10"
+
+# when uv/pip invoke maturin through the PEP 517 interface, it always builds in release mode by default.
+[build-system]
+requires = ["maturin>=1.9.6,<2.0"]
+build-backend = "maturin"
+
+[tool.maturin]
+module-name = "rustbpe"
+bindings = "pyo3"
+manifest-path = "Cargo.toml"
+features = ["pyo3/extension-module"]
discussion_comments.comment_body:
This feature is needed if the `rustbpe`, as a member of the worksapce, is to be installed in editable mode (which is the default mode). 
Or we should specify `rustbpe` in the top `pyproject.toml` to be installed in  noneditable mode. 

On the other hand, this feature is needed, 
-- as you mentioned, if the package is to be published on PyPI, 
-- and for a docker image of the project, if any:
For a smaller image size, the `Dockerfile` often uses a separate build stage, possibly with a different `FROM` and Python version. Without the `extension-module` feature, the module is dynamically linked to the build stage's `libpython`, which won't exist in the final image, causing a runtime error. With the feature, the module is not linked to `libpython`, and its symbols are resolved at runtime by the host Python process that loads it.

Thank you very much for your review. 
Yes, the dependencies are needed for the feature.

And I just set the versions to match the `maturin`'s version in `rustbpe/pyproject.toml` (actually `pyo3` should be `0.27`, but `pyo3-log` still does not support it)
```
requires = ["maturin>=1.9.6,<2.0"]
```
Is it OKs so?

discussion_comments.comment_body:
ROCm 6.4 is the latest supported one, and there's also ROCm 7. I suggest switching to TheRock wheels for best compatibility https://github.com/ROCm/TheRock/blob/main/RELEASES.md#installing-pytorch-python-packages - these also install ROCm itself as pip wheels, so you don't need to worry about whether the system contains a specific version of ROCm. 

EDIT: Currently TheRock wheels are device-specific, so maybe it's best to stick with 6.4/nightly 7.0 wheels from the official pytorch index until TheRock releases with multi-device wheels.
